field_mismatches.arbitration: Filing says the Company countersigned Barclays engagement letter on April 19, 2016. Alex's precise date of 2016-03-09 does not correspond to any Barclays engagement event; Alex's rough date of 2016-04-15 is close (the April 15 Board meeting authorized the engagement). Pipeline's 2016-04-19 is filing-grounded and correct.

matched_pairs.arbitration: Pipeline date is correct per filing. Alex recorded the precise date incorrectly as 2016-03-09 (which is the Thoma Bravo IOI date). Alex's rough date of 2016-04-15 tracks the Board authorization date, not the countersigning date.

field_mismatches.arbitration: These represent different events. The pipeline's Target Sale on 2016-05-05 is the Board's launch of the strategic process. Alex's Bidder Sale on 2016-03-09 codes Thoma Bravo's unsolicited approach as initiating a sale process. Both are defensible framings but refer to different moments in the narrative. The filing supports both: Thoma Bravo's March 9 IOI triggered the Board's eventual May 5 strategic process launch.

Pipeline dates the target_sale to May 5, 2016 (Board formally authorized the process). Alex dates the Bidder Sale to March 9 (Thoma Bravo's unsolicited letter). Both are filing-grounded but code different trigger concepts.

field_mismatches.arbitration: Pipeline classifies the $19.25 best-and-final as Informal because the filing calls it a 'revised written, non-binding proposal.' Alex classifies it as Formal because it came after the final round deadline and was the decisive closing bid. Filing says 'non-binding' but it was clearly solicited by the process letter's best-and-final mechanism. Pipeline's enrichment labels it Informal based on the 'non-binding' signal; Alex's Formal classification reflects the post-final-round, best-and-final nature. Alex's coding is the more conventional M&A classification for a best-and-final offer requested via process letter.

pipeline_only.grounding_verdict: Filing-grounded. Filing says 'In early 2015, and again in June 2015, representatives of Thoma Bravo informally approached and had brief meetings.' Pipeline extracted this as a separate June 2015 event. Alex collapsed all pre-IOI Thoma Bravo interest into BidderID 0.5 (Bidder Interest with rough date 2016-01-31). Pipeline is more granular.

Filing-grounded. Filing says 'In January 2016, representatives of Thoma Bravo met with members of Company management during an industry conference and reiterated Thoma Bravo's interest.' Alex captured this as part of BidderID 0.5. Pipeline granularity is superior.

Filing-grounded. The July 8, 2016 final bid deadline is explicit in the filing. Alex recorded a Final Round row on June 24 (the announcement date, BidderID 17) but did not create a separate row for the July 8 deadline itself. Pipeline's separation of announcement vs. deadline is more precise.

Filing-grounded. Filing says 'Later on July 13, 2016, before the stock market opened, the Company and Thoma Bravo issued a joint press release.' Alex does not have a separate Bid Press Release row. This is a coverage difference; pipeline is more granular.

Filing-grounded. Filing says 'In early 2015 ... representatives of Thoma Bravo informally approached.' This is the earliest undated approach. Alex consolidated this into BidderID 0.5. Pipeline is more granular, treating each separate approach as its own event.

alex_only.grounding_verdict: Partially filing-grounded but conflates multiple timeline events. Alex uses BidderID 0.5 as a composite Bidder Interest row with precise date 2016-03-09 (the March 9 IOI date) and rough date 2016-01-31 (end of January 2016 meeting). The pipeline extracts three separate bidder_interest events (early 2015, June 2015, January 2016) plus a separate proposal for March 9. Alex's approach merges pre-IOI interest and the IOI into one row, losing temporal resolution. Filing supports the pipeline's more granular decomposition.

Weakly filing-grounded. Alex treats the June 3 bid instruction letter (which set a June 9 deadline for first-round IOIs) as a 'Final Round Inf Ann' -- an informal final round announcement. The filing describes this as the initial bid instruction letter for first-round indications of interest, not a final round. The pipeline does not extract this as a separate event because it is not a final-round construct. Alex's taxonomy maps the first-round IOI deadline to informal final round terminology. The filing does not call this a 'final round.'

Weakly filing-grounded. Alex codes the June 9 first-round IOI deadline as 'Final Round Inf' (informal final round deadline). Filing says the Board 'approved requesting initial non-binding indications of interest from bidders by June 9, 2016.' This is a first-round IOI deadline, not a final round. Pipeline does not model this as a final_round event. Alex's taxonomy interpretation differs from the filing's characterization.

Weakly filing-grounded. Alex codes a 'Final Round Ann' at the June 9/June 12 boundary, apparently representing the Board's June 12 decision to advance three parties to the second phase. The filing describes this as the Board authorizing advancement to the second phase, not as a formal final round announcement. The pipeline's Final Round Ann on June 24 (when Barclays sent formal final bid process letters) is the more filing-accurate coding of the formal final round announcement.

Not filing-grounded. Alex codes a 'Final Round Ext Ann' (extension round announcement) on June 24. The filing does not describe any extension of the final round. June 24 is when the original final bid process letters were sent with a July 8 deadline. There was no prior deadline that was being extended. The pipeline correctly models this as the single Final Round Ann.

Not filing-grounded. Alex codes a 'Final Round Ext' (extension deadline) with precise=June 24 and rough=July 8. The filing does not describe any extension. The July 8 deadline was the original and only final bid deadline. There was no extension. This row appears to be an artifact of Alex's taxonomy mapping the two-phase process (informal first round + formal final round) onto a round-extension model.

key_disagreements.description: Alex models the process with informal final rounds (Final Round Inf Ann/Inf at June 3/June 9) and extension rounds (Final Round Ext Ann/Ext at June 24/July 8). Pipeline models a single formal final round (Ann June 24, deadline July 8). Filing supports the pipeline's simpler model: the June 3-9 period was a first-round IOI solicitation, not a final round.

Pipeline codes Target Sale on May 5 (Board launches process). Alex codes Bidder Sale on March 9 (Thoma Bravo's unsolicited IOI). Both are filing-defensible but represent different conceptual framings.

summary.status_rationale: Match rate of 79.3% (23 matched out of 29 max rows) falls in the 70-90% attention range. No pipeline claims are contradicted by the filing. Two Alex date fields are filing-contradicted. The most substantive disagreement is the bid_type classification for the $19.25 best-and-final offer and the round taxonomy differences.
